segments.s485-g17: همان نیز = 'that also/that too' (anaphoric demonstrative +  نیز). بزم آمدت is a past construction where آمدت = آمد + enclitic -ت meaning 'came to you / befell you' (past); بزم as a noun ('feast, assembly') is the thing that befell. رزم کید is a noun‑phrase 'battle and plot/stratagem' placed after the verbal phrase to describe the kind of thing that occurred. The sequence lists the state that befell the addressee: a revel/assembly and a scheming of war.
segments.s485-g18: بر آنی که = a temporal/causal phrase: بر (on/upon) + آنی ('that instant / moment') + که ('that/when'), here functioning like 'at the moment when' or 'when/because'. شاهانت is the referent whose fate is described; گشتند is the past plural of گشتن 'to become/turn' and takes the plural subject indicated by the verb; صید is a predicative noun 'prey' showing what they became. Word order is verb-final poetic, so 'گشتند صید' = 'they became prey.'
segments.s485-g19: برین‌گونه = a frozen/poetic compound meaning 'in this manner/thus'; عنوان = 'title' or 'heading' (here: the kind of title/topic). بر + ین + سان together form a stacked adjectival/specifying phrase (بر = on/upon, ین = the -īn form marking 'this/that' in older orthography, سان = 'like'), and سخن = 'speech/words'. The whole segment enumerates 'a title/phrase of this sort.'
segments.s485-g20: نیامد = negative past 'did not come' (here impersonal: 'did not befit / did not reach'). به‌ما = 'to/for us' (dative). زان = 'from that' (indicating source). کیان = 'the Kayanian/kings (the old royal house)' and کهن = adjective 'ancient/old.' Together: 'Such words did not come to us from those old Kayanian kings.'
segments.s485-g21: منم = 'I am' / 'I myself' (emphatic). The repeated فور (an emphatic/poetic word meaning 'sudden surge/haste') with وز between them produces a terse, rhythmic qualificative (فور وز فور) emphasizing swiftness/force; دارم = present 'I have/possess'; نژاد = 'lineage / descent'. Grammatically, the adjectival/adverbial cluster (فور وز فور) modifies دارم نژاد: 'I possess an ancestry marked by sudden force/energy' (poetic self‑praise).
segments.s485-g22: که = 'for / that' introducing the explanatory clause. از = 'from / among' (source). قیصران = 'the Caesars / emperors'. کس = 'anyone / person' (here indefinite). نکردیم = past negative 'we did not do / we did not (have)'; with یاد = 'memory / recollection', the clause means 'we do not have any recollection of anyone from the Caesars' — i.e., 'there was no one like that among the Caesars.'
segments.s485-g23: بدانگه = 'at that time / thereupon' (temporal adverb). که = 'that / when' introducing a subordinate clause. دار here is a poetic noun (one common sense is 'gallows' or 'place of hanging' but can mean 'abode'); مرا = accusative clitic 'me'; یار = 'companion, friend'; خواست = past 'wanted/desired'. The clause reads 'when the dar sought me as a companion' — i.e., poetically: 'when doom/the gallows claimed me.'
segments.s485-g24: دل و بخت = 'heart and fortune' (two nouns joined). با او = 'with him/it' (indicating accompaniment or support). ندیدیم = past negative 'we/I did not see / did not find'. راست here is an adverbial intensifier 'truly / indeed'. The clause therefore says 'I truly found neither heart nor luck with him/it,' i.e. no courage or fortune sided with that companion/doom.
segments.s485-g25: همی is an archaic/emphatic particle here (used before a verb) giving an emphatic or continuous flavour to the action. ژنده‌پیلان is a plural direct object (ژنده‌پیل + -ان) placed before the verb, and فرستادمش is a simple past transitive verb (فرستادم “I sent”) with the enclitic object -مش (= him/her/it); the subject (I) is omitted as usual in Persian poetry.
segments.s485-g26: همیدون is treated as a proper name (no ezāfe shown). The preposition به introduces the beneficiary/target of دادمش; بازی زمان is a genitival/construct-like phrase (play/game of Time) with the ezāfe omitted as is common in verse, and دادمش is again past tense 'I gave it/him' with the enclitic -مش referring back to the same object/person.
segments.s485-g27: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). بر دست functions poetically as an instrumental phrase 'by the hand (of…)'; آن bindeh-bar is the noun 'that slave‑taker' and کشته شد is a passive construction (کشته 'killed' + شد as auxiliary 'was'), so the clause means 'who was killed by the hand of that slave‑taker.'
segments.s485-g28: سرِ بخت is a fused phrase ('head/chief of fortune'); ایرانیان is the possessed/agent (the Iranians). گشته شد is a compound passive/perfect-like phrase (گشته 'become/turned' + شد 'was'), giving the sense 'the Iranians' fortune was turned/overturned.' Word order (noun + participle + شد) is typical of poetic passive/resultative expressions.
segments.s485-g29: گر is the conditional 'if'. او را uses the object/topic marker را after the pronoun (emphatic/accusative). ز دستور بد sets up a cause ('from/because of a bad command'); the second بد is predicate/adverbial ('ill/badly') and رسید is a past intransitive 'reached/befell'—together 'if bad (ill) befell him from a bad command.'
segments.s485-g30: چرا is 'why'. شد here functions as 'happened/became' introducing the resultant state; خرد is the subject 'wisdom', در سرت is a locative phrase 'in your head' (در + سرت), and ناپدید is the predicate 'vanished/was gone' — 'why did wisdom in your head vanish?'.
segments.s485-g31: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. در جنگ is a locative adjunct 'in battle'. چندین دلیری is a quantified noun phrase 'many/several acts of bravery' (چندین + noun), and مکن is the negative imperative/optative 'do not (do)'. The verb appears at the end as is normal in Persian imperatives.
segments.s485-g32: که introduces the reason ('for/because'). با مات is 'with the defeated' (با + مات, مات here = 'defeated'), کوتاه باشد is a subjunctive/predicative clause 'may be short' or simply 'is short' in poetic register, and سخن ('speech/words') is the subject in inverted order — 'for with the vanquished, speech is brief.'
segments.s485-g9: مرا پیش خوانی: م + را together form the object 'مرا' (me) where 'را' is the object marker; it precedes the verb, a common poetic word order. خوانی is 2nd-person singular present 'you call / summon' (here 'to summon'), and the subject 'تو' is expressed by the verb ending (the enclitic/ending represented in the next tokens). ترا شرم نیست follows as a separate clause: 'ترا' (to/for you, formed here from تو + را) is the person affected, and 'شرم نیست' literally = 'shame is not' → 'you have no shame.'
segments.s485-g10: خرد را بر مغزت آزرم نیست: 'خرد را' is the direct object 'wisdom' marked by را; 'بر مغزت' is a prepositional phrase 'upon your brain' (مغز + possessive -ت). آزرم is the noun/predicate being negated by نیست — here functioning as the quality that is lacking (the manuscript's lexicon marks this word as obscure/proper), so syntactically the clause reads 'there is no (آزرم) upon your brain regarding wisdom' → 'you have no scruple/pity about putting wisdom on your head' (rendered more naturally above).
segments.s485-g11: اگر فیلقوس این نوشتی به فور: اگر is the conditional 'if'; فیلقوس is a proper name (subject). این is the proximal demonstrative 'this' placed before the verb as the object. نوشتی is a past/perfect form 'you wrote' (second-person suffix implied by form). به فور is an adverbial phrase 'immediately/at once' modifying نوشتی; به functions adverbially with فور.
segments.s485-g12: تو نیز آن هم آغاز و بردار شور: تو نیز 'you also'; آن هم آغاز = 'begin that too' where آغاز (literally 'beginning') is used as an exhortation/verb ('begin'). و بردار شور: و 'and', بردار is an imperative 'take (up)' and شور is the noun 'tumult/ardor/strife' (object), so the whole phrase is a two-part imperative: 'begin that as well and take up the tumult.'
segments.s485-g13: ز دارا بدین سان شده‌ستی دلیر: ز 'from/because of' introduces the cause ('دارا' = wealth/the wealthy). بدین سان is the adverbial 'in this way' (بدین = 'by this/so', سان = 'manner'). شده‌ستی is 2nd-person perfect 'you have become', and دلیر is the predicate complement 'brave'. Word order places the causal and manner phrases before the verb and complement, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s485-g14: کزو گشته بد چرخ گردنده سیر: کزو 'from which / from it' refers back to the preceding idea; گشته is a perfect/participial form 'has become / has turned'; بد is an adjective 'bad / ill‑favored' qualifying چرخ (here 'wheel' → fate/sky); گردنده is a participle/adjective 'rotating / revolving'; سیر (here) means 'course / circuit' or 'journey'. Together the clause says that from that (situation) the ill‑turning revolving wheel of fate has taken its course — a poetic way to say the wheel of fate now moves ill for someone.
segments.s485-g15: چو بر تخمه‌ای بگذرد روزگار: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بر تخمه‌ای is a prepositional phrase 'upon a scion / offspring' (تخمه‌ای = a seed/offspring, indefinite with -ی); بگذرد (subjunctive/present) is 'passes' (time passes over someone), and روزگار is the subject 'time / fate' — ordering places the verb before the subject for poetic effect.
segments.s485-g16: نسازند با پند آموزگار: نسازند is 3rd-person plural present/jussive negative of ساختن 'to make/form/fit', here meaning 'they do not make/shape/agree'; با پند آموزگار is the complement 'with the teacher's advice' (با = with; پند = advice; آموزگار = teacher). The clause therefore says they will not be reformed/affected by a teacher's counsel.
segments.s485-g33: ببینی is the present-subjunctive/optative (second-person) form of دیدن used here as an exhortation 'behold/see'. It is followed by the temporal adverb کنون ('now'), then a compound noun phrase ژنده پیل و سپاه ('the ragged elephant and the host'), where the modifier ژنده precedes the noun پیل in the usual Persian modifier→noun order; و simply joins پیل and سپاه.
segments.s485-g34: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'which/that'. پیشت is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'before you/in front of you' (prepositional element + enclitic -ت). ببندند is third-person plural present-subjunctive/jussive used after که to report what those forces do ('they close/they block'). بر here governs باد + راه; باد is likely the noun 'wind' in the phrase بر باد راه ('the road toward/for the wind' or 'the road to ruin'), and راه is the noun 'road/path' — word order is somewhat poetic but functions as object/complement of ببندند.
segments.s485-g35: همی is an archaic/prosodic particle meaning 'still/always' placed before the clause. رای تو is a possessive noun phrase 'your counsel/plan' (noun before possessive pronoun in poetry). برترین گشتن is an infinitival/verbal phrase 'to become foremost/supreme' (گشتن = 'to become' here), and the sentence-final است is the copula 'is', so the structure is 'It is your counsel always to become foremost.'
segments.s485-g36: نهان تو places the adjective/nominal نهان ('hiddenness/secret') before the pronominal تو ('your') — a compact poetic expression meaning 'what is hidden of you' or 'your secrecy'. چون is the comparative particle 'like', رنگ is 'color/appearance', آهرمن is the proper name Ahriman (a devil), and the sentence-final است is the copula 'is'. The clause reads 'your hiddenness is like the color of Ahriman.'
segments.s485-g37: به گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'in the world' (به here functions as 'in/with' in classical usage). همه modifies تخم ('entirely/wholly a seed'), and تخم زفتی is a noun phrase 'a foul/nasty seed' (تخم = 'seed/origin', زفتی = 'filthy, nasty'). مکار at the end addresses the addressee as 'deceitful one' or predicates 'be not deceitful' — the exact imperative/character-labeling reading is determined by the surrounding exhortation.
segments.s485-g38: بترس is the second-person singular imperative 'fear'/'beware'. از introduces the objects of fear; گزند means 'harm', و links the two nouns, and بد روزگار is a fixed collocation 'ill-fortune / bad times' (بد modifies روزگار). The whole clause is an admonition: 'fear harm and ill-fate.'
segments.s485-g39: بدین نامه is a prepositional phrase 'by / with this letter' (here split in the wordlist into ب + دین but read together as بدین). ما is the subject 'we', and نیکویی خواستیم is past-tense first-person-plural 'we sought/wished for goodness' (خواستیم = 'we wanted/sought'). Word order is topic (instrument) → subject → verb phrase, typical of formal/poetic Persian.
segments.s485-g40: منقش modifies دلت ('your heart'); note that ت is the 2nd-person enclitic on دل (دل + ت = دلت 'your heart'). را is the definite object marker attached after the possessed noun, and بیاراستیم is a past-tense first-person-plural verb 'we adorned/arrayed' (in context 'we have ornamented/engraved'). The clause reads 'we adorned/engraved your heart.'
segments.s485-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; آن نامه is the object 'that letter'; برخواند is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb 'read aloud/recited'. فور is an adverb 'immediately' and here precedes the following noun (سترگ) to signal promptness ('immediately, his eyes...'). Word order: temporal marker + object + verb, then adverb + noun continuing into the next clause.
segments.s485-g2: برآشفت is an intransitive past (3rd sg.) meaning 'became upset/grew agitated' or 'was angered'; here it is the main verb of the clause. زان functions as a demonstrative determiner ('that' / 'from that') pointing to the person being described, followed by the epithets نامدار بزرگ 'renowned, great' describing that person. The noun phrase (زان نامدار بزرگ) is the subject that bears the verbal state.
segments.s485-g3: هم‌آنگه is a temporal adverb 'at that moment/thereupon'; یکی is an indefinite subject 'one / a certain (person)'; تند modifies پاسخ and here means 'sharp/quick' (an adverbial or adjectival sense); پاسخ نوشت is verb phrase 'wrote a reply' with نوشت in past 3rd-person. Typical Persian order: subject + modifiers + object + verb.
segments.s485-g4: به here indicates location/direction 'into/in' (i.e., in the orchard); پالیز is 'orchard'; کینه درختی is a genitival phrase 'a tree of hatred' (کینه modifies درختی); بکشت is the past tense 'he planted' (archaic b‑ prefix before the verb stem), placed at the end as the clause's verb.
segments.s485-g5: سر نامه is a fixed phrase meaning 'the head/opening of the letter' (سر = top/heading); گفت is the narrative past 'said' (introducing direct speech or the letter's wording); از introduces source/agent here ('from' or 'by'), so از خداوند پاک reads 'from/by the Pure Lord.'
segments.s485-g6: بباید is a modal 'must/ought'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'; باشیم is the present-subjunctive/obligative 'we be / we should be' governed by بایست/بباید; با ترس و باک is a prepositional phrase 'with fear and dread,' where the two nouns are joined by و as synonyms reinforcing the feeling.
segments.s485-g7: نگوییم is a 1st-person-plural negative jussive/optative 'let us not say' (or 'we should not say'); چندین سخن means 'many/several words'; بر گزاف is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in vain/needlessly' where گزاف is adjectival 'groundless/vain' and بر indicates the manner ('in/with vain'). The phrase functions as direct object of نگوییم.
segments.s485-g8: که introduces the result/consequence clause 'that/so that/lest'; بیچاره is a predicate adjective 'unfortunate/wretched'; باشد is the subjunctive/present 'may be / would be' marking potential/result; خداوند لاف is a nominal phrase 'the boastful lord' (خداوند here as 'lord/sovereign' and لاف 'boast' as epithet, i.e. 'the one given to boasting').

sentences.s485-s9: That too became for you a gathering of revelry and a plot of battle, at the moment when your kings were turned into prey.
sentences.s485-s10: Words of that kind and title did not come to us from those ancient Kayanian kings.
sentences.s485-s11: I myself possess noble lineage in sudden force and surge; for we do not recall anyone like that among the Caesars.
sentences.s485-s12: At that time when the gallows (or doom) sought me as a companion, truly I found neither heart nor fortune with him.
sentences.s485-s13: I sent the ragged elephants, and I gave Hamīdūn over to Time's sport.
sentences.s485-s14: who was killed by the hand of that slave‑taker; the Iranians' fortune was overturned.
sentences.s485-s15: If ill befell him from a bad command, why did wisdom vanish from your head?
sentences.s485-s16: You, in battle, do not commit many acts of bravery, for with the defeated words are brief.
sentences.s485-s5: You summon me forward — you have no shame; you have no shame of placing wisdom upon your brain.
sentences.s485-s6: If Filqus wrote this at once, you too should begin that as well and take up the tumult.
sentences.s485-s7: Because of wealth you have thus become brave; from it the ill‑turning, revolving wheel of fate has taken its course.
sentences.s485-s8: When time passes over a scion (a seed/offspring), they are not shaped by the admonition of the teacher.
sentences.s485-s17: Now behold the ragged elephant and the host that, before you, close up the road to the wind.
sentences.s485-s18: Your counsel is ever to become foremost; your hiddenness is like the color of Ahriman.
sentences.s485-s19: In the world, do not be wholly a foul seed, O deceiver; fear harm and ill fate.
sentences.s485-s20: By this letter we sought goodness; we adorned (engraved) your heart.
sentences.s485-s1: When that letter was read, his eyes at once flared up, and that famed great man grew agitated.
sentences.s485-s2: Immediately thereafter a certain man wrote a sharp reply and planted a tree of hatred in the orchard.
sentences.s485-s3: At the head of the letter it said: 'From the Pure Lord it is necessary that we be with fear and dread.'
sentences.s485-s4: Let us not speak many vain words, lest the boastful lord be made miserable.
